content: Compound interest is a powerful force that can significantly grow your savings over time. In simple terms, it's the idea that the interest you earn on your investments can itself earn interest, creating a snowball effect. Think of it like a rolling snowball, gathering size and speed as it goes. The longer your money is invested, the more time it has to grow exponentially. For example, if you invest $1,000 at a 5% annual interest rate, you'll have $1,050 after one year. But in the second year, you'll earn 5% interest on the new total of $1,050, not just the original $1,000. This means you'll earn $52.50 in interest, rather than just $50. Over time, this difference can add up to thousands of dollars, making compound interest a valuable tool for achieving long-term financial goals.
